title: Acute Shortness of Breath - COPD Exacerbation

doorInstructions.scenario: Mr. Mohammed, 72-year-old smoker with known COPD, presents with worsening shortness of breath and increased sputum production for 3 days.
doorInstructions.task: Take a focused history, perform respiratory examination, request appropriate investigations, and provide a diagnosis with management plan.
doorInstructions.vitals_given: HR 110, BP 140/85, RR 28, SpO2 88% on RA, Temp 38.1°C

patient.name: Mr. Mohammed Ali
patient.appearance: Sitting upright, using accessory muscles, pursed-lip breathing, audible wheeze

history.openingStatement: Doctor, I can't breathe... I've been getting worse over the last few days. My inhalers aren't helping like they usually do.

onset.response: It started about 3 days ago. I had a cold last week and then my breathing just got worse and worse.

symptoms.response: I'm coughing up a lot of greenish-yellow phlegm, more than usual. I also feel feverish and my chest feels tight.

breathlessness.response: I get breathless even at rest now. I can't walk to the bathroom without gasping. I have to sleep sitting up in my chair.

inhaler_use.response: I've been using my blue inhaler every hour but it's not helping much. I also take Spiriva every morning but I might have missed a few doses.

past_medical.response: I've had COPD for 10 years. I was hospitalized last year for the same thing. I also have high blood pressure.

medications.response: I take Spiriva and Symbicort every day, Ventolin as needed, and amlodipine for blood pressure. I finished a course of prednisone last month.

smoking.response: I smoked 2 packs a day for 45 years. I quit 5 years ago when they told me I had COPD, but I still crave them.

allergies.response: No allergies to medications. I've taken antibiotics before for chest infections.

home_oxygen.response: No, I don't use oxygen at home. My doctor mentioned it last time but I didn't want it.

vaccination.response: I got the flu vaccine this year but I'm not sure about the pneumonia one.

review_of_systems.response: No chest pain, but my ankles are a bit swollen. I haven't been eating much because I feel too breathless to chew.

general.inspection: Alert, sitting upright in tripod position, using accessory muscles (sternocleidomastoid), pursed-lip breathing, audible expiratory wheeze, tachypneic at 28/min

respiratory.inspection: Barrel-shaped chest, increased AP diameter, equal but reduced chest expansion bilaterally, intercostal and supraclavicular retractions
respiratory.palpation: Reduced tactile fremitus bilaterally, trachea midline
respiratory.auscultation: Diffuse expiratory wheeze bilaterally, prolonged expiratory phase, decreased breath sounds at bases, coarse crackles at right base

cardiovascular.palpation: Apex beat displaced to epigastrium (hyperinflation), no thrills
cardiovascular.auscultation: Tachycardic at 110, S1 S2 normal but distant (hyperinflation), no murmurs

extremities.inspection: Mild bilateral ankle edema, warm extremities, no clubbing, no cyanosis

abg.name: Arterial Blood Gas
abg.finding: pH 7.31, PaCO2 65, PaO2 55, HCO3 32, SpO2 88% - Acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis with hypoxemia

chest_xray.name: Chest X-Ray
chest_xray.finding: Hyperinflated lungs, flattened diaphragms, increased retrosternal air space, no pneumothorax, patchy opacity at right lower lobe suggesting consolidation

cbc.name: Complete Blood Count
cbc.finding: WBC 15,200 (neutrophils 82%), Hb 15.8, Platelets 280,000 - Leukocytosis with left shift

sputum_culture.name: Sputum Culture & Sensitivity
sputum_culture.finding: Pending - results in 48-72 hours. Gram stain shows many neutrophils and gram-negative rods.

ecg.name: ECG
ecg.finding: Sinus tachycardia at 110 bpm, right axis deviation, P pulmonale (right atrial enlargement), no acute ischemic changes

diagnosis.primary: Acute Exacerbation of COPD with Right Lower Lobe Pneumonia

immediate: Oxygen therapy to target SpO2 88-92% (controlled O2 to avoid CO2 retention)
Systemic corticosteroids (Prednisolone 40mg PO or Hydrocortisone IV)
Antibiotics (Amoxicillin-Clavulanate or Doxycycline) for community-acquired pneumonia
Consider non-invasive ventilation (NIV/BiPAP) if no improvement or worsening respiratory acidosis
monitoring: Repeat ABG in 1-2 hours after initiating treatment
Monitor for NIV response or need for intubation
longTerm: Home oxygen assessment if persistent hypoxemia

items.description: Asked about onset and duration of symptoms
Asked about cough, sputum character and color
Assessed severity of breathlessness (at rest vs exertion)
Asked about inhaler use and response
Asked about COPD history and previous exacerbations
Asked about medications and recent steroid use

globalRating.fail: Inadequate - critical assessment items missed, potential patient harm
globalRating.borderline: Some critical items missed, needs improvement in respiratory assessment
globalRating.good: Good performance, systematic approach to respiratory emergency
globalRating.excellent: Outstanding, comprehensive assessment and management of acute respiratory failure

bonusItems: Maintained calm demeanor during respiratory distress
Asked about advance directives or escalation plans
penaltyItems: Did not assess severity of respiratory distress promptly
Failed to recognize need for urgent intervention
Did not position patient optimally for breathing
Forgot to check oxygen delivery system
